task_name: Academic Research Presentation - Non-invasive Brain Imaging of Laparoscopic Motor Skills Learning

frame_analyses.description: Title slide showing 'Non-invasive brain imaging of laparoscopic motor skills learning' by Arun Nemani, PhD
Introduction to FLS as validated testing platform showing images of all 5 FLS tasks
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Visual overview of peg transfer, pattern cutting, endoloop, extracorporeal suture, and intracorporeal suture tasks

frame_analyses.description: Discussion of current FLS metrics limitations and challenges
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Highlights poor interrater reliability and focus on speed over accuracy

demonstrated_techniques: Introduction to FLS as standardized assessment platform
Overview of all 5 FLS tasks with visual examples
Discussion of NIRS brain imaging for objective skill assessment
Experimental setup using pattern cutting task with novices and experts
Brain activation mapping showing differences between skill levels
verbal_cues: FLS is required for board certification
See one, do one, teach one is not ideal for motor skills training
Current metrics have poor interrater reliability
NIRS can objectively differentiate surgical motor skills in real time
common_errors_addressed: Over-reliance on time-based metrics that may sacrifice accuracy
Lack of objective assessment methods for surgical skill evaluation
Need for real-time feedback during skill acquisition

confidence_rationale: This is clearly an academic research presentation about brain imaging during FLS tasks, not an actual FLS performance to be scored. Content is educational/instructional in nature.
technique_summary: Academic research presentation discussing use of near-infrared spectroscopy (NIRS) to objectively assess laparoscopic motor skill acquisition through brain imaging during FLS pattern cutting tasks.
